skill.name: hugging-face-cli
skill.description: Use the Hugging Face `hf` CLI to download Hub assets, upload repositories, manage cache, create repos, and operate jobs or endpoints.
skill.summary: Run Hugging Face Hub workflows through the official `hf` CLI.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution and blocker patterns, but review shows they are markdown examples for the Hugging Face `hf` CLI rather than embedded executable code. The skill is still medium risk because it instructs users to run networked commands that can upload files, delete Hub resources, handle tokens, start jobs, and deploy endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Operations Affect Remote Hub Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to run `hf` commands that download from and upload to Hugging Face Hub, create or delete repositories, manage jobs, and deploy endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but mistakes can publish local files, remove remote assets, or create paid infrastructure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly show upload, delete, job, and endpoint commands. The behavior is legitimate for the tool, but it creates real operational risk if run without review.

medium_findings.title: Token Handling in Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The authentication and jobs examples include token-based login and passing `HF_TOKEN` as a secret. This is legitimate, but users must avoid exposing tokens in shell history, logs, prompts, or shared transcripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly references token login, token secrets, and token override options. No exfiltration is present, but credential exposure is a realistic misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Upload and Cache Modification Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands that upload current directories, sync a Space with local files, download models to local paths, and remove cached repositories. These commands can disclose local content or remove useful cached data if paths are chosen incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows commands that read, upload, download, or delete local filesystem content. The risk depends on user-selected paths and repository targets.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across the file. Review found markdown inline code and fenced bash examples, not executable Ruby code embedded in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only file is markdown and the flagged syntax appears in documentation tables or fenced code blocks. There is no script file or automatic execution wrapper.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the cited lines describe the `hf` CLI, endpoint descriptions, or skill metadata. No cryptographic implementation or weak hash use was found in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary documentation text and command names, not crypto APIs. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or related weak cryptography use.

low_findings.title: Environment Information Command Is User-Initiated Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The skill lists `hf env` and `hf auth whoami` for troubleshooting account and environment state. This can reveal local configuration when a user runs it, but the skill does not automatically collect or transmit the output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit user-run diagnostics. They may expose local state in logs, but there is no hidden execution or exfiltration path in the markdown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Remote Delete Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The command reference includes repository, branch, tag, file, and endpoint deletion examples. Users should confirm targets and prefer dry-run or pull-request workflows where available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The delete commands are directly visible and can affect remote resources. They are normal CLI features, but dangerous if invoked against the wrong repository or endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Current Directory Upload Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use `hf upload <repo_id> . .` and Space sync with deletion. This can upload unintended files or overwrite remote content if users do not review includes, excludes, and target paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact upload and sync patterns are present in the file. The risk is contextual, but the commands can affect broad file sets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Compute and Endpoint Provisioning
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for jobs and inference endpoint deployment. These operations may consume paid infrastructure or expose services if users run them without checking account, region, and access settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands start jobs, deploy endpoints, and name GPU flavors. They are intentional Hub features with billing and exposure implications.

content.user_title: Manage Hugging Face Hub from the CLI
content.value_statement: Hugging Face Hub workflows often require many precise commands. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused reference for safe `hf` CLI operations.
actual_capabilities: Lists common `hf` commands for authentication, downloads, uploads, repository management, and cache operations.
Shows examples for downloading models, datasets, Spaces, and selected files from Hugging Face Hub.
Provides upload patterns for model repositories, datasets, and Spaces, including pull request and commit message options.
Covers repository creation, branch and tag management, file deletion, and repository settings changes.
Includes examples for Hugging Face Jobs, inference endpoints, and GPU flavor selection.
Documents useful options such as repo type, revision, token override, and quiet output.
limitations: It is a command reference, not a wrapper that validates commands before execution.
It does not verify account permissions, billing status, or organization policy before suggesting commands.
It does not install the Hugging Face CLI or authenticate the user automatically.
It does not inspect local files before upload commands that target Hub repositories.

use_cases.title: Download a model for local deployment
use_cases.description: Find the right `hf download` command, select a revision, and place files in a local model directory.

use_cases.title: Publish a trained artifact to the Hub
use_cases.description: Create a private repository, upload output files, and tag a release with clear CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Operate Hub infrastructure from scripts
use_cases.description: Use jobs, endpoint, cache, and authentication commands while keeping tokens and destructive actions visible.

prompt_templates.title: Download a model
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the safest `hf` CLI command to download a Hugging Face model into a local folder. Ask for the repo ID if needed.

prompt_templates.title: Upload a dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me upload a local dataset folder to a private Hugging Face dataset repository. Include checks for target repo, path, and commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Clean local cache
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Hugging Face cache cleanup using `hf cache` commands. Show what to inspect before removing cached revisions.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and manage an endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a cautious `hf endpoints` workflow to deploy, inspect, pause, resume, and delete an inference endpoint. Include confirmation steps.

output_examples.input: User asks how to download a model to `./model`.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for the repo ID, recommends checking license and size, then provides an `hf download` command with a local directory.

output_examples.input: User wants to publish training output to a private model repository.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines repo creation, local file review, upload with a commit message, and optional release tagging.

output_examples.input: User needs to stop paying for an unused endpoint.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests listing endpoints, describing the target, pausing or scaling to zero first, and deleting only after confirmation.

best_practices: Review local paths and ignore patterns before any upload or sync command.
Use private repositories and pull requests when publishing early or sensitive artifacts.
Keep Hugging Face tokens out of prompts, logs, shell history, and shared transcripts.
anti_patterns: Running broad upload commands from a repository root without checking files first.
Deleting repositories, files, tags, or endpoints without confirming the namespace and target name.
Passing long-lived tokens directly in visible command lines when safer secret handling is available.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Hugging Face commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance for the user or agent to review and run in the active environment.

faq.question: Can it upload private or sensitive files?
faq.answer: Yes, upload commands can publish selected local files. Review paths, excludes, and repository visibility before running them.

faq.question: Does it require a Hugging Face token?
faq.answer: Many authenticated operations require a token. Use secure secret handling and avoid exposing token values in prompts or logs.

faq.question: Can it manage datasets and Spaces?
faq.answer: Yes. The command reference includes repo type options for models, datasets, and Spaces.

faq.question: Can it start paid compute resources?
faq.answer: Yes. Jobs and endpoints can use remote compute. Confirm billing, GPU flavor, region, and cleanup steps first.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it documents powerful networked CLI operations.
